questions.text: Shape of CO2 is similar to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: SnCl2 has a bent shape due to the presence of a lone pair on the central atom, which differs from the linear shape of CO2.
BF3 has a trigonal planar shape, which is not similar to the linear shape of CO2.
BeF2 has a linear shape due to the arrangement of electron pairs around the central atom, similar to CO2.
H2O has a bent shape due to lone pairs on the oxygen atom, which is different from the linear shape of CO2.

questions.explanation: The shape of CO2 is linear, as it has two double bonds with oxygen atoms that are arranged symmetrically around the central carbon atom. According to the VSEPR theory, this results in a linear shape because there are no lone pairs on the central atom to cause any bending. The correct answer is BeF2, which also has a linear shape due to a similar arrangement of bonds around the central beryllium atom. In contrast, SnCl2 and H2O have bent shapes due to lone pairs on the central atoms, and BF3 has a trigonal planar shape due to its three bonding pairs arranged in a plane.

questions.text: Shape of NH3 is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Linear molecules have a bond angle of 180 degrees, typical of molecules like CO2. NH3 is not linear due to the presence of a lone pair on nitrogen.
The shape of the ammonia molecule (NH3) is trigonal pyramidal due to the sp3 hybridization of nitrogen, resulting in three bonded pairs and one lone pair of electrons.

Planar molecules have atoms arranged in a single plane, often seen in molecules like BF3. NH3 is not planar as the lone pair on nitrogen pushes the hydrogen atoms below the plane, creating a pyramidal shape.
Angular or bent shapes occur in molecules like H2O, where the bond angle is reduced due to lone pairs. NH3 has a pyramidal shape, not angular.

questions.explanation: The shape of the ammonia molecule (NH3) is trigonal pyramidalThe trigonal pyramidal shape of ammonia is due to the fact that the nitrogen atom is sp3 hybridized. This means that the nitrogen atom has four equivalent orbitals that are directed towards the corners of a tetrahedron. The three hydrogen atoms bond to the nitrogen atom through these sp3 orbitals.

questions.text: When the two partially filled atomic orbital overlap in such a way that the probability of -finding the electron is maximum around the line joining the two nuclei, the result is the formation of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Sigma bonds are formed by the head-on overlap of two atomic orbitals, resulting in a bond with electron density concentrated along the line connecting the two nuclei. This type of overlap is more effective and results in a stronger bond.

Pi bonds are formed by the sideways overlap of atomic orbitals, with electron density above and below the line joining the nuclei. They are generally weaker than sigma bonds and do not fit the description of the head-on overlap mentioned in the question.

A hydrogen bond is an intermolecular force, not a bond formed through the overlap of atomic orbitals. It occurs between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to an electronegative atom and another electronegative atom with a lone pair of electrons.

An ionic bond is formed through the electrostatic attraction between positively and negatively charged ions, not by the overlap of atomic orbitals. This is a fundamentally different bonding process compared to covalent bonding.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Sigma bond. A sigma bond is formed when two atomic orbitals overlap head-on, creating a region of electron density directly along the line connecting the two atomic nuclei. This type of overlap is typical for single covalent bonds and is the strongest covalent bond due to maximum orbital overlap.Option B is incorrect because a pi bond results from the sideways overlap of orbitals, which occurs in addition to a sigma bond in double or triple bonds, rather than alone in single bonds.Option C is incorrect because hydrogen bonds are not formed by orbital overlap, but rather by an interaction between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom and another electronegative atom.Option D is incorrect because ionic bonds are formed by the electrostatic attraction between ions, not by orbital overlap.

questions.text: pi bond is formed sideways overlap of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: s orbitals are spherical and cannot overlap sideways to form a pi bond. They are involved in forming sigma bonds through head-on overlap.

To form a pi bond, the p-orbitals on the two atoms must be parallel to each other and overlap sideways. This creates a region of high electron density above and below the bond axis, holding the atoms together in a pi bond.

d orbitals can participate in bonding, but they are not typically involved in the formation of pi bonds through sideways overlap. They are more common in complex bonding scenarios, such as in transition metals.
This option is incorrect as pi bonds are indeed formed through the sideways overlap of orbitals, specifically p orbitals.

questions.explanation: To form a pi bond, the p-orbitals on the two atoms must be parallel to each other and they must overlap in such a way that the lobes of the orbitals are on opposite sides of the bond axis. The overlap of the p-orbitals creates a region of high electron density above and below the bond axis. This region of high electron density is what holds the two atoms together in a pi bond.

questions.text: Which has sp3 hybridization?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The central carbon atom in methane (CH4) is sp3 hybridized. This means that the carbon atom's 2s orbital and 3p orbitals combine to form four equivalent sp3 orbitals, resulting in a tetrahedral shape.
The carbon atoms in acetylene (C2H2) are sp hybridized, as they form a triple bond with each other, using one s and one p orbital to form two equivalent sp orbitals.
In propylene (C3H6), the central carbon atoms are sp2 hybridized due to the presence of a double bond, utilizing one s and two p orbitals to form three equivalent sp2 orbitals.

The central carbon atom in ethanol (C2H5OH) is indeed sp3 hybridized. However, since the question asks for a single compound with sp3 hybridization, CH4 is the simplest and most straightforward example.

questions.explanation: The central carbon atom in methane (CH4) is sp3 hybridized. This means that the carbon atom's 2s orbital and 3p orbitals combine to form four equivalent sp3 orbitals

questions.text: Which exhibits hydrogen bonding?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: HI does not exhibit hydrogen bonding because iodine is not sufficiently electronegative to form a hydrogen bond. Additionally, iodine is too large to effectively participate in hydrogen bonding.
NH3 exhibits hydrogen bonding because nitrogen is a highly electronegative atom and has a lone pair of electrons, allowing it to form hydrogen bonds with hydrogen atoms of other ammonia molecules.
SiH4 does not exhibit hydrogen bonding because silicon is not sufficiently electronegative and lacks the necessary lone pair of electrons to participate in hydrogen bonding.
Not all the molecules listed exhibit hydrogen bonding. Only NH3 is capable of hydrogen bonding, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Here are the conditions for hydrogen bonding to occur:1) A hydrogen atom must be covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom. The most common electronegative atoms that can form hydrogen bonds are oxygen, nitrogen, and fluorine.2) The electronegative atom must have a lone pair of electrons. The lone pair of electrons on the electronegative atom can form a hydrogen bond with the hydrogen atom of another molecule.

questions.text: Which has dative bond?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CCl4 or carbon tetrachloride consists of four covalent bonds where carbon shares electrons with chlorine. There is no donation of lone pairs, hence no dative bond.
CH4 or methane has purely covalent bonds. Carbon shares electrons with hydrogen atoms, and there is no lone pair donation involved, so no dative bond.
In carbon monoxide (CO), the oxygen atom donates a pair of electrons to carbon, forming a dative covalent bond. This is in addition to the usual covalent bonds, making CO unique in this regard.
CO2, or carbon dioxide, consists of double covalent bonds where electrons are shared equally between carbon and oxygen atoms. There is no donation of lone pairs, so no dative bond exists.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is CO. In carbon monoxide, the oxygen atom donates a lone pair of electrons to the carbon atom, forming a dative covalent bond. This type of bond is characterized by one atom providing both electrons for the bond formation. Other options like CCl4, CH4, and CO2 do not have this characteristic, as they form regular covalent bonds through equal sharing of electrons.

questions.text: Which of the following has the highest ionization energy?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Aluminum (Al) is in period 3, group 13. It has a lower ionization energy compared to the other options because it is located towards the left side of its period, where ionization energies are generally lower.

Sulfur (S) is in period 3, group 16. It has a relatively high ionization energy due to its smaller atomic size and higher effective nuclear charge compared to aluminum and silicon, but it is still lower than phosphorus.
Silicon (Si) is in period 3, group 14. Its ionization energy is higher than that of aluminum, but lower than sulfur and phosphorus due to its intermediate position in the group.

Phosphorus (P) is in period 3, group 15. It possesses the highest ionization energy among the options provided, as it is located further to the right in the periodic table compared to aluminum, silicon, and sulfur.

questions.explanation: Phosphorus (P) has the highest ionization energy among the options given because it is located in group 15 of period 3, where ionization energy increases due to a higher effective nuclear charge and smaller atomic radius compared to the other elements listed.Aluminum (Al) has the lowest ionization energy because it is in group 13. Silicon (Si) has a higher ionization energy than aluminum but is still lower than phosphorus because it is further left in the periodic table. Sulfur (S), while having a higher ionization energy than silicon and aluminum, does not exceed that of phosphorus due to its position in group 16, which is still to the left of phosphorus.

questions.text: Addition of a second electron to a uni-negative ion is always _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When a second electron is added to a uni-negative ion, the incoming electron is repelled by the already present negative charge, and energy is absorbed in this process. It is an endothermic process and the absorbed energy is given a positive sign. So, electron affinity is positive.O + e- → O1- EA1 = -141 kJ/molO- + e- → O2- EA2 = +744 kJ/mol.
It is an endothermic process. Hence, it is an incorrect option.
It is not a neutral process instead an endothermic process. Hence, it is an incorrect option.
Energy is absorbed and not wasted hence it is an incorrect option.

questions.explanation: When a second electron is added to a uni-negative ion, the incoming electron is repelled by the already present negative charge, and energy is absorbed in this process. It is an endothermic process and the absorbed energy is given a positive sign. So, electron affinity is positive.O + e- → O1- EA1 = -141 kJ/molO- + e- → O2- EA2 = +744 kJ/mol.

questions.text: Those elements, with an electronic configuration of valence shell ns2 np6 show little tendency to react chemically, are called?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reactivity of lanthanides is generally lower than that of the corresponding alkali and alkaline earth metals. Lanthanides are relatively unreactive with water and oxygen, and they do not readily dissolve in most acids. However, they can react with strong oxidizing agents, such as chlorine or fluorine, to form a variety of compounds

Actinides are highly reactive elements due to their large atomic and ionic radii, high charge densities, and the presence of f-orbitals, which make them more susceptible to oxidation and reduction reactions.
The alkali metals are highly reactive metals, and their reactivity increases from top to bottom of the group

Noble metals include metals such as gold, silver, platinum, and palladium. These metals are characterized by their low chemical reactivity, which is due to the fact that they have a completely filled outermost electron shell

questions.explanation: The reactivity of lanthanides is generally lower than that of the corresponding alkali and alkaline earth metals. Lanthanides are relatively unreactive with water and oxygen, and they do not readily dissolve in most acids. However, they can react with strong oxidizing agents, such as chlorine or fluorine, to form a variety of compounds. Actinides are highly reactive elements due to their large atomic and ionic radii, high charge densities, and the presence of f-orbitals, which make them more susceptible to oxidation and reduction reactions. The alkali metals are highly reactive metals, and their reactivity increases from top to bottom of the group. Noble metals include metals such as gold, silver, platinum, and palladium. These metals are characterized by their low chemical reactivity, which is due to the fact that they have a completely filled outermost electron shell.

questions.text: The correct order of atomic radii is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is an incorrect option. The correct order is Li < K < F. The atomic radius generally decreases as we move from left to right across a period in the periodic table. Lithium (Li) has the largest atomic radius among the three elements, followed by potassium (K), and then fluorine (F) with the smallest atomic radius. So, the correct order is indeed Li < K < F.

Correct! F is on the extreme right side of the periodic table, so it has the smallest size. Li, Na, and K are alkali metals with larger sizes. Their size increases down the group, so the correct order of atomic size is F < Li < Na < K.

This option is incorrect. The order of atomic radii Li < Na < K < F is wrong. The atomic radius generally increases as we move down a group in the periodic table. So, the correct order should be Li < Na < K > F. Lithium (Li) has a smaller atomic radius than sodium (Na), which in turn has a smaller atomic radius than potassium (K). However, fluorine (F) has the smallest atomic radius among these elements.

This option is incorrect. The order of atomic radii Na < K < Li < F is wrong. The atomic radius generally increases as we move down a group in the periodic table. So, the correct order should be Li < Na < K > F. Lithium (Li) has a smaller atomic radius than sodium (Na), which in turn has a smaller atomic radius than potassium (K). However, fluorine (F) has the smallest atomic radius among these elements.

questions.explanation: The correct order of atomic radii is F < Li < Na < K. Fluorine (F) has the smallest atomic radius due to its position on the extreme right side of the periodic table. On the other hand, lithium (Li), sodium (Na), and potassium (K) are alkali metals with increasing atomic radii down the group. Therefore, the correct order is F < Li < Na < K. The incorrect options either reverse the order or place elements incorrectly based on their positions in the periodic table.

questions.text: Which one of the following is smallest in size?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Na+ is the smallest in size among the options provided. When Na loses an electron to become Na+, the reduced electron-electron repulsion allows the remaining electrons to be pulled closer to the nucleus, making it smaller than the neutral Na atom.
O2- is larger than a neutral O atom because gaining two electrons increases electron-electron repulsion and expands the electron cloud, making it larger in size.
F- is larger than a neutral F atom due to gaining an electron, which increases electron-electron repulsion and expands the electron cloud, resulting in a larger size.
N3- is larger than a neutral N atom because gaining three electrons significantly increases electron-electron repulsion and expands the electron cloud, leading to a larger size.

questions.explanation: When ions form by gaining or losing electrons, their sizes are influenced by the resulting changes in electron-electron repulsion and the distribution of the electron cloud. Na+ is the smallest in size as it loses an electron, reducing repulsion and allowing electrons to be closer to the nucleus. O2-, F-, and N3- all become larger due to gaining electrons, which increases repulsion and expands the electron cloud.

questions.text: Consider the iso-electronic species,K+,S2-,Cl- and Ca2+, the radii of the ion decrease as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. When an atom loses electrons to become a positive ion, like Ca2+ and K+, the ion size decreases. However, when an atom gains electrons to become a negative ion, like Cl- and S2-, the ion size increases.
Incorrect. The correct order should consider the effects of gaining and losing electrons on ion size in iso-electronic species.
Incorrect. The order of ionic radii in iso-electronic species is influenced by the charges on the ions and their impact on the size of the ion.

Correct! When an atom gains electrons to become a negative ion, like S2- and Cl-, the ion size generally increases compared to the neutral atom. Conversely, when an atom loses electrons to become a positive ion, like K+ and Ca2+, the ion size generally decreases compared to the neutral atom.

questions.explanation: When comparing the iso-electronic species S2-, Cl-, K+, and Ca2+, the order of ionic radii from largest to smallest is S2- > Cl- > K+ > Ca2+. This is because when atoms gain electrons to form negative ions, the ion size increases, as seen in S2- and Cl-. On the other hand, when atoms lose electrons to form positive ions, the ion size decreases, as observed in K+ and Ca2+. Therefore, the correct order considers the impact of the charges on the ions on their respective sizes.

questions.text: The radius of fluorine atom is 72pm and that of the fluoride ion (F) is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the radius of the neutral fluorine atom, not the fluoride ion.
Incorrect. This value is too small for a fluoride ion, which is larger than the neutral atom.
Correct. The fluoride ion is larger than the neutral fluorine atom due to electron-electron repulsion after gaining an additional electron.
Incorrect. This value is smaller than even the radius of a neutral fluorine atom.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 133pm. When a fluorine atom gains an electron to become a fluoride ion (F-), the increased electron-electron repulsion causes the ion to expand, resulting in a larger radius than the neutral atom. Option A (72pm) is incorrect as it represents the neutral fluorine atom's radius. Option B (44pm) and Option D (36pm) are incorrect because they are both smaller than the radius of the fluoride ion.

questions.text: One of the following species is iso-electronic with Ca2+. Which is that
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Strontium ion (Sr2+) is not iso-electronic with Ca2+. Iso-electronic species have the same number of electrons, but Sr2+ has more electrons than Ca2+. Strontium ion has 54 electrons, while calcium ion (Ca2+) has 20 electrons.
Sodium atom (Na) is not iso-electronic with Ca2+. Sodium atom has 11 electrons, while calcium ion (Ca2+) has 20 electrons.
Argon atom (Ar) is iso-electronic with Ca2+. Both argon atom and calcium ion (Ca2+) have the same number of electrons, which is 18.
Magnesium ion (Mg2+) is not iso-electronic with Ca2+. Magnesium ion has 10 electrons, while calcium ion (Ca2+) has 20 electrons.

questions.explanation: Atomic No= No. of electronsC=20 electrons,Ca+2=20 - 2 = 18 electrons18Ar= 18 electronsThe correct answer is C) Ar - Argon atom. Argon atom (Ar) is iso-electronic with Ca2+ as both have the same number of electrons, which is 18.The other options are incorrect because they do not have the same number of electrons as Ca^2+.

questions.text: The radii of H, H+ and H- are in the order of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A suggests an incorrect order of radii. The correct order should be in increasing order from left to right (smallest to largest).
Option B suggests an incorrect order of radii. The correct order should be in increasing order from left to right (smallest to largest).

Option C is the correct answer. The correct order of radii is H- > H > H+. The hydride ion (H-) has the largest radius, followed by the hydrogen atom (H), and the hydrogen ion (H+) has the smallest radius.
Option D suggests an incorrect order of radii. The correct order should be in increasing order from left to right (smallest to largest).

questions.explanation: Cationic radius > Atomic radius > Anionic radius. The correct answer is C) H- > H > H+. The correct order of radii is H- > H > H+. The hydride ion (H-) has the largest radius, followed by the hydrogen atom (H), and the hydrogen ion (H+) has the smallest radius.

questions.text: The ionization energy of an element is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The energy released when an electron is added to an atom of the element
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron, not add it.

options.text: The same as the electron affinity of the element
options.explanation: Incorrect. Ionization energy is about removing electrons, while electron affinity is about adding electrons.

options.text: Equal in magnitude but of opposite sign to the electron affinity of the element
options.explanation: This is not correct. Ionization energy and electron affinity are related but not equal in magnitude or sign.

options.text: The energy required when electron is removed from outermost shell.
options.explanation: Correct! Ionization energy is indeed the energy needed to remove an electron from an atom's outermost shell.

questions.explanation: The ionization energy of an element is the energy needed to remove an electron from an atom's outermost shell. This is absorbed energy as opposed to being released. The other options are incorrect because they either involve adding electrons (not removing) or incorrectly relate ionization energy to electron affinity.

questions.text: The 1st ionization energy of metal is lower and 2nd ionization energy is very high, it indicate that group number is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A suggests that the metal belongs to Group 1 (IA) of the periodic table. In Group 1, the elements have one valence electron. The first ionization energy is relatively low because it is easy to remove the single valence electron to form a cation. However, the second ionization energy is much higher because removing an inner electron requires more energy.

Option B suggests that the metal belongs to Group 2 (IIA) of the periodic table. In Group 2, the elements have two valence electrons. The first ionization energy is relatively low because it is easy to remove one of the valence electrons to form a cation. However, the second ionization energy is higher than the first but still lower compared to Group 1 elements.

Option C suggests that the metal belongs to Group 3 (IIIA) of the periodic table. In Group 3, the elements have three valence electrons. The first ionization energy is relatively low because it is easy to remove one of the valence electrons to form a cation. However, the second ionization energy is higher than the first, but not as high as the second ionization energy of metals in Group 1.
Option D suggests that the group number is IVA (Group 4). However, the information given in the question does not match the properties of Group 4 elements.

questions.explanation: If there is a large gap between the 1st and 2nd ionization energy, it indicates that the metal contains one valence electron and belongs to Group 1 (IA). The correct answer is A) IA (Group 1). When the 1st ionization energy of the metal is lower and the 2nd ionization energy is very high, it confirms that the metal belongs to Group 1 (IA) of the periodic table. Options B, C, and D present alternative group numbers that do not align with the given ionization energy characteristics.

questions.text: Which one of the following series is arranged in order of increasing value:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The first ionization energies of: Oxygen, Fluorine, Neon
options.explanation: Option A is correct as it is arranged in order of increasing first ionization energies: Neon < Oxygen < Fluorine. Ionization energy increases from left to right across a period due to the increase in effective nuclear charge.

options.text: The radii of: H-ion, H atom, H+ ion
options.explanation: Option B is incorrect as it refers to atomic radii, not ionization energies. The correct order for atomic radii is H+ ion < H atom < H-ion.

options.text: The electro negativities of: Chlorine, Bromine, Iodine
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect as it discusses electronegativities, not ionization energies. The correct order for electronegativities is Iodine < Bromine < Chlorine.

options.text: The boiling points of: Iodine, Bromine, Chlorine
options.explanation: Option D is incorrect as it relates to boiling points, not ionization energies. The correct order for boiling points is Chlorine < Bromine < Iodine.

questions.explanation: Ionization energy increases from left to right in a period due to the increase in effective nuclear charge (Zeff), resulting in higher energy required to remove an electron. Therefore, the correct answer is A) The first ionization energies of: Oxygen, Fluorine, Neon. The series is arranged in order of increasing first ionization energies: Neon < Oxygen < Fluorine.Options B, C, and D are incorrect as they discuss different atomic properties like atomic radii, electronegativities, and boiling points, respectively, rather than ionization energies.

questions.text: The unit of ionization energy is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A suggests that the unit of ionization energy is joule. This is correct. Ionization energy is measured in joules (J) as it represents the energy required to remove electrons from atoms or ions.
Option B suggests that the unit of ionization energy is newton. However, newton is a unit of force and not used to measure ionization energy.
Option C is correct. KJ/mol stands for kilojoules per mole, a common unit used to express ionization energy. It represents the energy required to remove electrons from atoms or ions.
Option D suggests that the unit of ionization energy is kilojoules. However, the standard unit is KJ/mol (kilojoules per mole) to express the energy required to remove electrons from atoms or ions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) KJ/mol - Kilojoules per mole. Ionization energy is indeed measured in kilojoules per mole, representing the energy needed to remove electrons from atoms or ions. Option A and D are incorrect as joule and kilojoules without the 'per mole' part are not the standard units for ionization energy. Option B is incorrect as newton is a unit of force, not energy.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT true about electronegativity:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: EN remains stable throughout a Group
options.explanation: Option A is correct. Electronegativity generally remains constant as you move down a Group in the periodic table. Elements within a Group have the same number of valence electrons and are in similar chemical environments, leading to similar electronegativity values.

options.text: With increase in O.S, EN increases
options.explanation: Option B suggests that electronegativity increases with an increase in oxidation state (O.S). This statement is generally true. Higher oxidation states are associated with a greater electron deficiency, leading to a stronger pull on shared electrons and higher electronegativity.

options.text: With increase in O.S, EN decreases
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect. Electronegativity actually increases with an increase in oxidation state. Higher oxidation states are typically associated with higher electronegativity values.

options.text: It determines the polarity of molecule
options.explanation: Option D is true. Electronegativity plays a crucial role in determining the polarity of a molecule. If there is a significant difference in electronegativity between two bonded atoms, the bond will be polar, resulting in partial charges on the atoms.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C) With an increase in oxidation state (O.S), EN decreases. This statement is not true, as with an increase in oxidation state, electronegativity usually increases. The correct option is A) EN remains stable throughout a Group. Electronegativity generally remains constant as you move down a Group in the periodic table.

questions.text: The greater 1st ionization energy would be associated with which of the following configuration
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A suggests the electron configuration of sodium (Na), which has a relatively low 1st ionization energy due to the ease of removing the outermost electron from the 3s orbital.
Option B suggests the electron configuration of nitrogen (N), which has a relatively high 1st ionization energy due to the removal of an electron from the half-filled 2p^3 subshell.
Option C suggests the electron configuration of fluorine (F), which has a relatively high 1st ionization energy due to removing an electron from the half-filled 2p^5 subshell.

Option D suggests the electron configuration of neon (Ne), which has the greatest 1st ionization energy among the given configurations as it requires more energy to remove an electron from its stable 2p^6 subshell.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6. Neon (Ne) has the greatest 1st ionization energy among the given configurations as it requires more energy to remove an electron from its stable 2p^6 subshell. Options A, B, and C have lower 1st ionization energies compared to neon because they belong to elements with different electron configurations and ionization energy trends.

questions.text: In the following, the element with the highest ionization energy is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This electron configuration belongs to nitrogen (N), which is not the element with the highest ionization energy in this set of options.
This electron configuration belongs to oxygen (O), which is not the element with the highest ionization energy in this set of options.

This electron configuration belongs to fluorine (F), which has the highest ionization energy among the given options because of its small atomic size and high effective nuclear charge, making it more difficult to remove an electron.
This electron configuration belongs to neon (Ne), which is not the element with the highest ionization energy in this set of options.

questions.explanation: The element with the highest ionization energy is fluorine (F) with the electron configuration [Ne] 3s2 3p3. Fluorine has the highest ionization energy due to its small atomic size and high effective nuclear charge, making it more difficult to remove an electron. This extra stability is attributed to the half-filled p-subshell in group V elements. Therefore, option C is correct. Options A, B, and D correspond to nitrogen, oxygen, and neon, respectively, and do not exhibit the highest ionization energy in this context.

questions.text: The power of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons towards itself in a molecule is called;
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Electron affinity is the energy change that occurs when an atom gains an electron to form a negatively charged ion.
Electronegativity is the power of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons towards itself in a molecule. It is a measure of the tendency of an atom to attract electrons in a chemical bond.

Ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from an atom to form a positive ion (cation). It is not directly related to the power of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons towards itself in a molecule.

Hydration energy is the energy released when gaseous ions are dissolved in water to form hydrated ions. It is not directly related to the power of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons towards itself in a molecule.

questions.explanation: Electronegativity is the correct answer.It refers to the power of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons towards itself in a molecule. This concept is essential in understanding the behavior of elements in chemical reactions. Option A, Electron affinity, is incorrect as it relates to the energy change when an atom gains an electron. Option C, Ionization energy, is incorrect as it pertains to the energy required to remove an electron from an atom. Option D, Hydration Energy, is also incorrect as it involves the energy released during the dissolution of gaseous ions in water.

questions.text: Which one of the following elements has greater electronegativity value?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Oxygen (O) is a highly electronegative element with an electronegativity value of 3.44.
Bromine (Br) is electronegative but not as electronegative as fluorine.
Fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element with an electronegativity value of 3.98.
Carbon (C) has a relatively low electronegativity value compared to other elements.

questions.explanation: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity value of 3.98, making it the correct answer. Electronegativity is the ability of an atom to attract bonding electrons. Oxygen, bromine, and carbon have lower electronegativity values compared to fluorine.

questions.text: Which statement is correct
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Loss of valence electron may lead to loss of valence shell
options.explanation: When an atom loses its valence electron, especially in elements with only one electron in the outermost shell, it can result in the loss of that entire shell, thereby reducing the atomic radius significantly.

options.text: Radius of isoelectronic ions decreases left to right in 3rd period
options.explanation: Isoelectronic ions have the same number of electrons. However, as the nuclear charge increases from left to right in the periodic table, the radius decreases due to stronger attraction between the nucleus and the electrons.

options.text: Anionic radius increases with magnitude of negative charge
options.explanation: As an atom gains more electrons and becomes more negatively charged, the repulsion between electrons increases, causing the anionic radius to expand.
All the statements provided about atomic and ionic radii are correct based on the principles of atomic structure and periodic trends.

questions.explanation: All the options describe accurate trends or phenomena related to atomic and ionic radii:Option A: The loss of a valence electron can indeed lead to the loss of the valence shell, particularly if there was only one electron in that shell, causing a decrease in atomic radius.Option B: Isoelectronic ions, despite having the same number of electrons, have radii that decrease as the nuclear charge increases across a period, due to stronger nucleus-electron attractions.Option C: Anions with more negative charges have larger radii due to increased electron-electron repulsion within the same shell.Option D: This option is correct as it encompasses all the true statements given in the other options.

questions.text: Which of the following element has greater negative first electron affinity (EA1)?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbon has the greater negative first electron affinity (EA1). The first electron affinity (EA1) refers to the energy change when an atom gains its first electron to form a negatively charged ion.
Lithium's first electron affinity is positive, meaning it requires energy to add an electron.
Boron's first electron affinity is also positive, meaning it requires energy to add an electron.
Beryllium's first electron affinity is also positive, meaning it requires energy to add an electron.

questions.explanation: The first electron affinity (EA1) refers to the energy change when an atom gains its first electron to form a negatively charged ion. In this case, Carbon has the greatest negative first electron affinity among the options given. This is because electron affinity generally increases along the period from left to right. Be < Li < B < C. Therefore, Option A (Carbon) is the correct answer. Options B, C, and D (Li, B, Be) have positive first electron affinities, indicating that they require energy to add an electron, making them incorrect choices.

questions.text: Which of the following group of elements have positive 1st electron affinity?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Group IA have negative first electron affinities, which means that they tend to lose electrons easily.
Group VIA have negative first electron affinities, which means that they tend to lose electrons easily.
Group IIA have negative first electron affinities, which means that they tend to lose electrons easily.
Group VIIA elements have positive first electron affinity. This means that these elements tend to gain electrons easily.

questions.explanation: Group VIIA elements have positive first electron affinity, indicating they tend to gain electrons easily. This is why option D is correct. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they have negative first electron affinities, meaning those groups tend to lose electrons easily.

questions.text: Which of the following halogens has comparatively smaller first electron affinity?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element and has a strong attraction for electrons, resulting in a high first electron affinity.
Iodine (I) has a smaller first electron affinity compared to fluorine, as it is less electronegative and has a lower tendency to attract an additional electron.
Bromine (Br) has a smaller first electron affinity compared to fluorine.
Chlorine (Cl) also has a smaller first electron affinity compared to fluorine.

questions.explanation: Electron affinity decreases down the group in halogens. The correct order for the halogens in terms of first electron affinity is: Cl > Br > F > I. Therefore, the correct answer is B) I - Iodine. Iodine has a smaller first electron affinity compared to fluorine due to its lower electronegativity, indicating a lower attraction towards an additional electron. This makes options A, C, and D incorrect as they have higher first electron affinities than Iodine.

questions.text: Which one of the following isoelectronic species has comparatively smaller size?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Argon (Ar) is the correct answer. As we move across a period in the periodic table, the atomic size generally decreases. Since Ar is located to the right of P, Cl, and S, it has a smaller size.
This option is incorrect. Ar is located to the right of P, indicating that Ar has a smaller size.
This option is incorrect. Ar is located to the right of Cl, suggesting that Ar has a smaller size.
This option is incorrect. Ar is located to the right of S, implying that Ar has a smaller size.

questions.explanation: Among the given isoelectronic species, Ar (argon) has a comparatively smaller size. As we move across a period in the periodic table, the atomic size generally decreases. Since Ar is located to the right of P, Cl, and S, it has a smaller size. Therefore, option A is correct.

questions.text: Mark the incorrect statement about ionization energy:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It is an index of metallic character
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Ionization energy tends to decrease as you move down a group in the periodic table, indicating that metals generally have lower ionization energies compared to non-metals.

options.text: Elements having stable electronic configuration have greater ionization energy
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Elements with stable electronic configurations (such as noble gases) tend to have higher ionization energies because they have a full valence shell, making it harder to remove an electron.

options.text: It is always an endothermic process
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Ionization always requires an input of energy because you are breaking the electrostatic attraction between the electron and the nucleus.

options.text: Elements having greater ionization energy have high electron affinity
options.explanation: Ionization energy and electron affinity are related but distinct concepts. Ionization energy measures the energy required to remove an electron, while electron affinity measures the energy change when adding an electron to form a negative ion. An element with high ionization energy may not necessarily have high electron affinity. Therefore, this statement is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Ionization energy and electron affinity are related but distinct concepts. Ionization energy measures the energy required to remove an electron from an atom or a positive ion, while electron affinity measures the energy change when adding an electron to form a negative ion. An element with high ionization energy does not necessarily have high electron affinity. Therefore, option D is correct.

questions.text: Atomic size of an element depends on:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The atomic size of an element is not solely dependent on the number of inner shells. Other factors, such as nuclear charge, also play a role.
While nuclear charge does influence atomic size, it is not the only factor. The number of inner electron shells is also significant.

This option is correct because the atomic size of an element depends on both the number of inner electron shells and the nuclear charge.- The number of inner electron shells affects the atomic size because as you move down a column in the periodic table, additional electron shells are added, increasing the size of the atom.- The nuclear charge, or the positive charge in the nucleus, also affects the atomic size. As the nuclear charge increases, it attracts electrons more strongly, making the atom smaller.
This option is incorrect because both the number of inner shells and nuclear charge impact the atomic size of an element.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the atomic size of an element depends on both the number of inner electron shells (option A) and the nuclear charge (option B).- The number of inner electron shells affects the atomic size because as you move down a column in the periodic table, additional electron shells are added, increasing the size of the atom.- The nuclear charge, or the positive charge in the nucleus, also affects the atomic size. As the nuclear charge increases, it attracts electrons more strongly, making the atom smaller.Option A and Option B are incorrect as they each only consider one of the factors influencing atomic size, while Option D is incorrect as it disregards both important factors.

questions.text: Which one of the following elements has less electronegativity value?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Nitrogen is to the right of Carbon in the same period, resulting in a higher electronegativity due to increased nuclear charge compared to Carbon.
Oxygen is further to the right than Nitrogen in the same period, having an even higher electronegativity due to its greater nuclear charge compared to Nitrogen.
Fluorine is the most electronegative element in the periodic table, being to the right of Oxygen and having the highest electronegativity value.

Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period. Carbon is to the left of all the other elements in the same period, resulting in a lower electronegativity due to fewer protons in its nucleus compared to the other elements given.

questions.explanation: Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period in the periodic table. Carbon, being to the left of all the other elements in the same period, has a lower electronegativity value because it has fewer protons in its nucleus compared to the other elements given. Therefore, option C is correct. Nitrogen, Oxygen, and Fluorine have higher electronegativity values due to their positions to the right of Carbon in the same period.

questions.text: Which of the following is polar molecule?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CO2 (carbon dioxide) is a linear molecule with two polar C-O bonds, but the dipole moments cancel out, making the molecule nonpolar overall.
In NO, the oxygen atom is more electronegative than the nitrogen atom, creating a partial negative charge on the oxygen and a partial positive charge on the nitrogen, making it a polar molecule.
BF3 (boron trifluoride) has three polar B-F bonds, but the molecule is trigonal planar and the dipole moments cancel out, making it nonpolar.
SO3 (sulfur trioxide) is a trigonal planar molecule with polar S-O bonds, but the dipole moments cancel out, making it nonpolar.

questions.explanation: A polar molecule is one in which there is an uneven distribution of electrons, leading to a partial positive charge on one end and a partial negative charge on the other. In NO, the oxygen atom is more electronegative than the nitrogen atom, so it pulls the shared electrons closer to itself, creating a partial negative charge on the oxygen and a partial positive charge on the nitrogen. Therefore, option b is correct. Options A, C, and D are nonpolar molecules due to the cancellation of dipole moments.

questions.text: Mark the incorrect statement about electronegativity (E.N).
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The maximum E.N is that of F element in the periodic table
options.explanation: This is correct; fluorine is the most electronegative element.

options.text: The minimum E.N is that of Cs element in the periodic table
options.explanation: This is also correct; cesium is the least electronegative element.

options.text: It is the property of an element in the isolated form
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect because electronegativity is a property that is meaningful in the context of a chemical compound, not in the isolated form of an element. Electronegativity is a measure of an atom's ability to attract electrons towards itself in a chemical bond when it is part of a compound.
This is correct; electronegativity is a relative measure and does not have a specific unit associated with it.

questions.explanation: The correct option is C. It is the property of an element in the isolated form.This statement is incorrect because electronegativity is a property that is meaningful in the context of a chemical compound, not in the isolated form of an element. Electronegativity is a measure of an atom's ability to attract electrons towards itself in a chemical bond when it is part of a compound.

questions.text: Consider the following applications of electronegativity (E.N):I. If the E.N difference between two bonded atoms is zero or less than 0.5, then the bond is said to be non-polar covalent bond.II. If the E.N difference between two bonded atoms is 1.7, then the bond is said to be 50% covalent and 50% ionic bond.III. If the E.N difference between two bonded atoms is 1.6 or more than 0.5, then the bond is said to be polar covalent bond.IV. If the E.N difference between two bonded atoms is greater than 1.7, then the bond is said to be covalent in nature.Which one of the above statements is Incorrect?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Statements I, II, and III are all correct explanations of the relationships between electronegativity differences and the nature of chemical bonds.
Statements I, II, and III are all correct explanations of the relationships between electronegativity differences and the nature of chemical bonds.

Statement IV is incorrect. If the electronegativity difference between two bonded atoms is greater than 1.7, then the bond is typically considered to be primarily ionic in nature, not covalent. A large electronegativity difference indicates that one atom has a much stronger attraction for the shared electrons, leading to the formation of ions.
Statements I, II, and III are all correct explanations of the relationships between electronegativity differences and the nature of chemical bonds. Only IV is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Statement IV is incorrect. If the electronegativity difference between two bonded atoms is greater than 1.7, then the bond is typically considered to be primarily ionic in nature, not covalent. A large electronegativity difference indicates that one atom has a much stronger attraction for the shared electrons, leading to the formation of ions. Statements I, II, and III correctly describe the relationship between electronegativity differences and the nature of chemical bonds, distinguishing between non-polar covalent, polar covalent, and ionic bonds.
